title: Venous Thromboembolism Prophylaxis
oneLiner: Prevents DVT/PE in hospitalised adults at risk; reduces mortality by ~60% when used appropriately with risk assessment.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Massive PE:** sudden collapse, syncope, shock, raised JVP, hypoxia despite high FiO₂. *Finding:* acute right heart strain (ECG: S1Q3T3, echo: dilated right ventricle). Do NOT start prophylaxis until massive PE ruled out.
- **Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) with thrombosis:** platelet count falls >50% from baseline 5–10 days after starting LMWH/unfractionated heparin (UFH). *Finding:* new thrombosis *despite* being on prophylaxis. Check platelet count before every new prescription and weekly thereafter.
- **Major bleeding from prophylactic dose:** any unexplained hypotension or drop in Hb >20 g/L. *Finding:* visible oozing from drains/sites, retroperitoneal or intracranial (altered GCS). Stop prophylaxis immediately, reverse with protamine if on UFH (not fully effective for LMWH).
sections.Presentation:
- **DVT (usually asymptomatic):** unilateral leg swelling, warmth, erythema, dilated superficial veins, calf tenderness. Homan’s sign is **unreliable** – do not use.
- **PE:** sudden dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis, syncope, unexplained tachycardia/tachypnoea.
- **Bleeding:** visible haematoma, haematuria, melaena, drop in BP, rising lactate.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Leg swelling:**
    - **Cellulitis** → erythema + fever + tenderness, bilateral? – likely bilateral heart failure.
    - **Ruptured Baker’s cyst** → swelling behind knee + calf, ultrasound shows cyst.
    - **Superficial thrombophlebitis** → palpable cord, localised.
- **Chest pain/dyspnoea:**
    - **Pneumonia** → fever, cough, focal chest signs, raised CRP.
    - **Pneumothorax** → sudden pleuritic pain, hyperresonance, tracheal shift.
    - **Myocardial infarction** → ECG changes, troponin rise, cardiac risk factors.
- **Splitter:** Unilateral leg swelling + tachycardia + pleuritic chest pain → think DVT/PE first. If no leg swelling + bilateral chest signs → pneumonia or heart failure.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Wells score for PE (calculate immediately; if >4 proceed directly to CTPA). For DVT: two-level Wells score (DVT unlikely ≤1 vs likely ≥2).
- **Bloods:** FBC (platelets baseline), coagulation screen (INR, APTT), U&Es, Cr (calculate eGFR for LMWH dosing). D-dimer only if low/intermediate probability Wells – if raised, proceed to imaging.
- **Imaging:**
    - **First-line:** Venous compression ultrasound (CUS) of proximal leg veins for DVT. CTPA for PE (if renal impairment, V/Q scan).
    - **Second-line:** MR venography/pulmonary angiogram rarely needed.
- **Special tests:** No routine screening for VTE in asymptomatic patients – only for clinical suspicion.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** Use NICE-recommended **Padua** (medical) or **Caprini** (surgical) risk assessment. Example: Padua ≥4 = high risk (prophylaxis indicated).
- **Never-miss test:** **Platelet count** before starting any heparin product (LMWH/UFH). If patient already on heparin, check FBC and clotting before each new script.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - Perform risk assessment (Padua/Caprini) + bleeding risk assessment.
    - If high VTE risk AND low bleeding risk → start LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) after checking renal function.
    - If high bleeding risk → mechanical prophylaxis only (TED stockings + intermittent pneumatic compression). Reassess daily.
    - Document in notes: “VTE risk assessment performed, plan for [drug/mechanical].”
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
    - Daily reassess bleeding risk and mobilisation status.
    - If epidural/spinal anaesthesia: time LMWH dose with catheter insertion/removal per local protocol.
    - Continue until patient is fully mobilising or no longer at risk (NICE suggests: 5–14 days for medical, until discharge for surgical; extended for certain cancers/orthopaedics).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - After orthopaedic hip/knee surgery: extended prophylaxis for 28–35 days (rivaroxaban or LMWH per local policy).
    - Cancer patients: consider extended prophylaxis (up to 28 days or as per local oncology guidelines).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin [check local policy] SC daily (standard dose e.g., 40 mg if eGFR >30). TED stockings bilateral (ensure correct size). Encourage early mobilisation.
    - Fluids: 1.5 L crystalloid/24h unless contraindicated.
    - Diet: as tolerated.
    - Observations: NEWS2 as per local frequency.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
    - **Enoxaparin** 40 mg SC once daily (standard). If eGFR <30 mL/min: **reduce to 20 mg SC once daily** *[check local renal protocol]*.
    - **Dalteparin** 5000 units SC once daily (if eGFR <30: *[check local policy – often no dose reduction]*).
    - **Tinzaparin** 4500 units SC once daily (safe in renal impairment but requires monitoring if eGFR <20).
    - **UFH (unfractionated)** 5000 IU SC twice or three times daily (use if severe renal impairment or HIT suspicion; monitor APTT at 6h after first dose).
- **Common alternatives:**
    - **Rivaroxaban** 10 mg PO daily (for orthopaedic surgery/medical patients; avoid if CrCl <15, monitor for bleeding).
    - **Fondaparinux** 2.5 mg SC daily (if HIT history; avoid if eGFR <20).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
    - Active bleeding or high bleeding risk (INR >1.5, platelet <50, recent haemorrhagic stroke).
    - Current heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) – avoid all heparins.
    - Spinal/epidural anaesthesia within 12h of LMWH dose.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
    - **Enoxaparin:** reduce dose if eGFR <30. *[Check local renal dosing policy]*.
    - **UFH:** no renal adjustment needed but requires APTT monitoring.
    - **Rivaroxaban:** avoid if severe hepatic impairment with coagulopathy.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Omitting the renal dose adjustment for enoxaparin** – standard dose in CrCL <30 leads to accumulation and major bleeding. Always check eGFR and drug chart before prescribing.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
    - **Major bleeding** (intracranial, retroperitoneal, GI): incidence ~0.5–1% with LMWH.
    - **HIT:** platelet drop >50% with/without thrombosis – stop all heparin, start fondaparinux or direct oral anticoagulant.
    - **Injection site haematoma** – minor but can be painful.
- **Chronic:**
    - **Post-thrombotic syndrome** if DVT develops despite prophylaxis (leg swelling, pigmentation, ulceration) – rare if prophylaxis works.
sections.Prognosis: - Appropriate prophylaxis reduces VTE risk by ~60% with only a small absolute increase in major bleeding (~0.3%). In-hospital VTE mortality is <1% with prophylaxis vs 10–15% without.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always use a formal risk assessment tool** (Padua for medical, Caprini for surgical) – document in admissions clerk-in and daily notes.
- **Reassess bleeding risk daily** – many patients start with high bleeding risk but later become eligible for chemical prophylaxis.
- **Combine mechanical + chemical** for highest-risk patients (e.g., hip fracture, major abdominal surgery) if no contraindications.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not skip the baseline platelet count** before starting heparin – you will miss HIT later.
- **Do not use TED stockings alone as sole prophylaxis** in high-risk patients – Cochrane review shows they add little benefit without chemical prophylaxis (unless bleeding risk).
- **Do not “one-size-fit” all LMWH doses** – always check renal function, especially in elderly/comorbid patients.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
    - Signs of DVT (sudden leg swelling, pain, redness).
    - Signs of PE (sudden chest pain, shortness of breath, coughing blood).
    - Signs of bleeding (large bruises, black stools, blood in urine, headache + vomiting).
- **Follow-up:** Outpatient VTE clinic if extended LMWH needed (e.g., cancer, orthopaedics). Ensure patient knows how to inject.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / MET / ICU if:**
    - Suspected massive PE (NEWS2 ≥5, hypotension SBP <90, hypoxia SpO₂ <90% on 15 L, high lactate >2).
    - Suspected HIT (platelet count fall >50% from baseline) – stop all heparin, contact haematology.
    - Major bleeding (Hb drop >20 g/L, need for transfusion, haemodynamic instability).
    - Anaphylaxis (rare) to LMWH – call crash team.

rawMarkdown:
## Venous Thromboembolism Prophylaxis
**One-liner:** Prevents DVT/PE in hospitalised adults at risk; reduces mortality by ~60% when used appropriately with risk assessment.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Massive PE:** sudden collapse, syncope, shock, raised JVP, hypoxia despite high FiO₂. *Finding:* acute right heart strain (ECG: S1Q3T3, echo: dilated right ventricle). Do NOT start prophylaxis until massive PE ruled out.
- **Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) with thrombosis:** platelet count falls >50% from baseline 5–10 days after starting LMWH/unfractionated heparin (UFH). *Finding:* new thrombosis *despite* being on prophylaxis. Check platelet count before every new prescription and weekly thereafter.
- **Major bleeding from prophylactic dose:** any unexplained hypotension or drop in Hb >20 g/L. *Finding:* visible oozing from drains/sites, retroperitoneal or intracranial (altered GCS). Stop prophylaxis immediately, reverse with protamine if on UFH (not fully effective for LMWH).

### Presentation
- **DVT (usually asymptomatic):** unilateral leg swelling, warmth, erythema, dilated superficial veins, calf tenderness. Homan’s sign is **unreliable** – do not use.
- **PE:** sudden dyspnoea, pleuritic chest pain, haemoptysis, syncope, unexplained tachycardia/tachypnoea.
- **Bleeding:** visible haematoma, haematuria, melaena, drop in BP, rising lactate.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Leg swelling:**
    - **Cellulitis** → erythema + fever + tenderness, bilateral? – likely bilateral heart failure.
    - **Ruptured Baker’s cyst** → swelling behind knee + calf, ultrasound shows cyst.
    - **Superficial thrombophlebitis** → palpable cord, localised.
- **Chest pain/dyspnoea:**
    - **Pneumonia** → fever, cough, focal chest signs, raised CRP.
    - **Pneumothorax** → sudden pleuritic pain, hyperresonance, tracheal shift.
    - **Myocardial infarction** → ECG changes, troponin rise, cardiac risk factors.
- **Splitter:** Unilateral leg swelling + tachycardia + pleuritic chest pain → think DVT/PE first. If no leg swelling + bilateral chest signs → pneumonia or heart failure.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Wells score for PE (calculate immediately; if >4 proceed directly to CTPA). For DVT: two-level Wells score (DVT unlikely ≤1 vs likely ≥2).
- **Bloods:** FBC (platelets baseline), coagulation screen (INR, APTT), U&Es, Cr (calculate eGFR for LMWH dosing). D-dimer only if low/intermediate probability Wells – if raised, proceed to imaging.
- **Imaging:**
    - **First-line:** Venous compression ultrasound (CUS) of proximal leg veins for DVT. CTPA for PE (if renal impairment, V/Q scan).
    - **Second-line:** MR venography/pulmonary angiogram rarely needed.
- **Special tests:** No routine screening for VTE in asymptomatic patients – only for clinical suspicion.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:** Use NICE-recommended **Padua** (medical) or **Caprini** (surgical) risk assessment. Example: Padua ≥4 = high risk (prophylaxis indicated).
- **Never-miss test:** **Platelet count** before starting any heparin product (LMWH/UFH). If patient already on heparin, check FBC and clotting before each new script.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - Perform risk assessment (Padua/Caprini) + bleeding risk assessment.
    - If high VTE risk AND low bleeding risk → start LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) after checking renal function.
    - If high bleeding risk → mechanical prophylaxis only (TED stockings + intermittent pneumatic compression). Reassess daily.
    - Document in notes: “VTE risk assessment performed, plan for [drug/mechanical].”
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
    - Daily reassess bleeding risk and mobilisation status.
    - If epidural/spinal anaesthesia: time LMWH dose with catheter insertion/removal per local protocol.
    - Continue until patient is fully mobilising or no longer at risk (NICE suggests: 5–14 days for medical, until discharge for surgical; extended for certain cancers/orthopaedics).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - After orthopaedic hip/knee surgery: extended prophylaxis for 28–35 days (rivaroxaban or LMWH per local policy).
    - Cancer patients: consider extended prophylaxis (up to 28 days or as per local oncology guidelines).
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin [check local policy] SC daily (standard dose e.g., 40 mg if eGFR >30). TED stockings bilateral (ensure correct size). Encourage early mobilisation.
    - Fluids: 1.5 L crystalloid/24h unless contraindicated.
    - Diet: as tolerated.
    - Observations: NEWS2 as per local frequency.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
    - **Enoxaparin** 40 mg SC once daily (standard). If eGFR <30 mL/min: **reduce to 20 mg SC once daily** *[check local renal protocol]*.
    - **Dalteparin** 5000 units SC once daily (if eGFR <30: *[check local policy – often no dose reduction]*).
    - **Tinzaparin** 4500 units SC once daily (safe in renal impairment but requires monitoring if eGFR <20).
    - **UFH (unfractionated)** 5000 IU SC twice or three times daily (use if severe renal impairment or HIT suspicion; monitor APTT at 6h after first dose).
- **Common alternatives:**
    - **Rivaroxaban** 10 mg PO daily (for orthopaedic surgery/medical patients; avoid if CrCl <15, monitor for bleeding).
    - **Fondaparinux** 2.5 mg SC daily (if HIT history; avoid if eGFR <20).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
    - Active bleeding or high bleeding risk (INR >1.5, platelet <50, recent haemorrhagic stroke).
    - Current heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) – avoid all heparins.
    - Spinal/epidural anaesthesia within 12h of LMWH dose.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
    - **Enoxaparin:** reduce dose if eGFR <30. *[Check local renal dosing policy]*.
    - **UFH:** no renal adjustment needed but requires APTT monitoring.
    - **Rivaroxaban:** avoid if severe hepatic impairment with coagulopathy.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Omitting the renal dose adjustment for enoxaparin** – standard dose in CrCL <30 leads to accumulation and major bleeding. Always check eGFR and drug chart before prescribing.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
    - **Major bleeding** (intracranial, retroperitoneal, GI): incidence ~0.5–1% with LMWH.
    - **HIT:** platelet drop >50% with/without thrombosis – stop all heparin, start fondaparinux or direct oral anticoagulant.
    - **Injection site haematoma** – minor but can be painful.
- **Chronic:**
    - **Post-thrombotic syndrome** if DVT develops despite prophylaxis (leg swelling, pigmentation, ulceration) – rare if prophylaxis works.

### Prognosis
- Appropriate prophylaxis reduces VTE risk by ~60% with only a small absolute increase in major bleeding (~0.3%). In-hospital VTE mortality is <1% with prophylaxis vs 10–15% without.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always use a formal risk assessment tool** (Padua for medical, Caprini for surgical) – document in admissions clerk-in and daily notes.
- **Reassess bleeding risk daily** – many patients start with high bleeding risk but later become eligible for chemical prophylaxis.
- **Combine mechanical + chemical** for highest-risk patients (e.g., hip fracture, major abdominal surgery) if no contraindications.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not skip the baseline platelet count** before starting heparin – you will miss HIT later.
- **Do not use TED stockings alone as sole prophylaxis** in high-risk patients – Cochrane review shows they add little benefit without chemical prophylaxis (unless bleeding risk).
- **Do not “one-size-fit” all LMWH doses** – always check renal function, especially in elderly/comorbid patients.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
    - Signs of DVT (sudden leg swelling, pain, redness).
    - Signs of PE (sudden chest pain, shortness of breath, coughing blood).
    - Signs of bleeding (large bruises, black stools, blood in urine, headache + vomiting).
- **Follow-up:** Outpatient VTE clinic if extended LMWH needed (e.g., cancer, orthopaedics). Ensure patient knows how to inject.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / MET / ICU if:**
    - Suspected massive PE (NEWS2 ≥5, hypotension SBP <90, hypoxia SpO₂ <90% on 15 L, high lactate >2).
    - Suspected HIT (platelet count fall >50% from baseline) – stop all heparin, contact haematology.
    - Major bleeding (Hb drop >20 g/L, need for transfusion, haemodynamic instability).
    - Anaphylaxis (rare) to LMWH – call crash team.
